title: Recent Results on Finite Group Lattice Gauge Theories
speaker: Sky Yang Cao
abstract: The rigorous study of spin systems such as the Ising model is currently one of the most active research areas in probability theory. In this talk, I will introduce one particular class of such models, known as lattice gauge theories (LGTs), and go over its origins, motivations, and then some recent results. Along the way, I will also try to highlight some of the key differences between LGTs and the usual spin systems. The general theme is that LGTs are spin systems with topological considerations.

proof_stages.label: Gibbs-measure and gauge-theory motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames lattice gauge theories as Gibbs measures arising both from spin-system probability and from discretizing continuum Yang-Mills theory, and identifies topology as the distinctive feature to be explained.

proof_stages.label: Finite-group lattice gauge theory definition
proof_stages.summary: A finite subgroup of a unitary group is fixed, configurations are group-valued edge variables, plaquette holonomies define the Wilson action, and finite-group models are positioned as edge-spin analogues of the Ising model.

proof_stages.label: Gauge-invariant phase-transition observables
proof_stages.summary: After recalling Ising magnetization as a phase-transition diagnostic, local gauge symmetry is shown to force local edge expectations to vanish, so Wilson loops and their area-law versus perimeter-law decay become the correct observables.

proof_stages.label: Large-beta Wilson-loop and correlation results
proof_stages.summary: Recent results are stated: first-order large-beta Wilson-loop asymptotics for finite groups, extensions to Higgs-type models, and exponential decay of correlations, with the asymptotic constants governed by the nearest nonidentity group elements.

proof_stages.label: Low-temperature defect expansion
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy expands around ground states, first via Ising domain walls and then via plaquette defects encoded as lattice differential forms for finite abelian gauge groups.

proof_stages.label: Abelian closed-two-form decomposition lemma
proof_stages.summary: The key deterministic lemma asserts that closed lattice two-forms whose support splits into sufficiently separated plaquette regions decompose uniquely into closed two-forms supported on the separate regions.

proof_stages.label: Nonabelian topological obstruction and repair
proof_stages.summary: For nonabelian groups the separated-support lemma fails because metric separation does not preclude linking, so the proof must be reformulated using fundamental-group and link-complement topology to impose the right nonlinking condition.

proof_stages.label: Critical-regime open problems
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion highlights open problems beyond the large-beta theory, including exact critical values, off-critical exponential correlation decay, and area-versus-perimeter behavior at criticality.

examples.name: Ising model as the reference spin system
examples.note: The Ising model makes the Gibbs formalism, phase transition order parameter, and low-temperature domain-wall expansion concrete, and the domain-wall discussion is explicitly used to motivate the analogous plaquette-defect expansion for gauge theories.

examples.name: Linked loops from knot theory
examples.note: The possibility of loops being far apart but still linked is used to generate the key idea that nonabelian support decomposition must be governed by unlinking rather than by metric separation alone.
examples.engagement_reason: linked loops from knot theory are used as an analogy for why distance is insufficient and the link-complement fundamental group may not factor, but no concrete knot calculation is carried out
